 Each battalion has 1,149 personnel (including specialists like doctors, engineers, and divers).
 Headed by a Director General (DG), appointed by the Central Government.When Can an FIR Be Filed Against NDRF Personnel?
 Dereliction of Duty (e.g., refusing rescue ops without justification).
 Corruption (demanding bribes for relief services).
 Violence Against Civilians (excessive force during evacuations).
 Equipment Misuse/Theft (selling relief supplies).
 Fake Rescues (staging operations for media/benefits).
 Applicable Laws for FIRs
 A. Under Disaster Management Act, 2005
 Section 54: Punishment for disobeying orders (1-year jail + fine).
 Section 52: False claims (2-year jail + fine).
 B. Under Indian Penal Code (IPC)
 IPC 166: Public servant disobeying law.
 IPC 332: Voluntarily hurting to deter public servant.
 IPC 409: Criminal breach of trust (theft of relief funds/supplies).
 IPC 153A: Promoting enmity during relief work.
 C. Under Other Laws
 Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988: For bribery (Section 7).
 Arms Act, 1959: If weapons misused during ops.
category: Disaster Management Division Rules and Regulations

title: Enforcement of the provisions of the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Act, 2020
description: The Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Act, 2020 (FCRA 2020) amends the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act, 2010, imposing stricter regulations on the receipt and utilization of foreign contributions by individuals, associations, and companies in India.

title: Section 220 of the Finance Act, 2018
description:
Section 220 of the Finance Act, 2018 deals with tax evasion, fraudulent claims, and violations under the Income Tax Act, 1961, particularly concerning foreign funding and undisclosed income. If an entity or individual violates these provisions, an FIR can be filed for criminal prosecution.Section 220, Finance Act, 2018 (for tax fraud/foreign funding violations).
 Relevant IPC Sections:
 420 (Cheating) – If false declarations were made.
 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) – If multiple parties involved.
 Section 37 of FCRA, 2010 (if foreign funds misused).

title: Section 236 of the Finance Act, 2016
description:
related to the Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act, 2015. If an individual or entity conceals foreign assets or income, an FIR can be filed for prosecution under this provision.Primary Section:
 Section 236, Finance Act, 2016 (for willful evasion of tax on foreign income/assets).
 Supporting Laws:
 Black Money Act, 2015 (Sections 50, 51, 54) – Penalties & prosecution for undisclosed foreign assets.
 IPC Sections:
 420 (Cheating) – If false declarations were made.
 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) – If multiple parties involved.
 201 (Destruction of Evidence) – If documents were hidden/tampered with.

title: The Citizenship Act, 1955.
description:
The Citizenship Act, 1955 regulates the acquisition, termination, and deprivation of Indian citizenship. Violations under this Act—such as fraudulent acquisition of citizenship, illegal stay by foreigners, or false declarations—can lead to criminal prosecution and FIR registration.Primary Sections:
 Section 9 (Termination of citizenship for acquiring foreign citizenship).
 Section 10 (Deprivation of citizenship obtained fraudulently).
 Section 14 (Penalty for false representation/concealment of facts).

title: The Citizenship(Amendment) Act, 2015.
description:
The Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2015 amended the Citizenship Act, 1955, primarily to simplify procedures for Overseas Citizens of India (OCI) Cardholders. However, violations related to fraudulent OCI registration, false declarations, or illegal benefits can lead to cancellation of OCI status and criminal prosecution.
 Primary Sections:
 Section 7D, Citizenship Act, 1955 (Cancellation of OCI for fraud/misrepresentation).
 Section 14 (Penalty for false representation – 5 years jail + fine).
 Supporting Laws:
 IPC Sections:
 420 (Cheating) – For forged documents.
 468 (Forgery) – Fake affidavits/certificates.
 471 (Using forged documents as genuine).
 Foreigners Act, 1946 (For visa/OCI misuse).

title: The Foreign (Contribution) Regulation Act, 2010.
description:
The FCRA, 2010 regulates foreign funding to NGOs, individuals, and associations in India. Violations include illegal foreign funding, misuse of funds, non-disclosure, or political activities using foreign contributions.
 Primary Sections:
 Section 11 (Prohibition to accept foreign contribution without registration).
 Section 12 (Conditions for grant of certificate).
 Section 35 (False statement in application – 6 months jail/fine).
 Section 37 (Misuse of funds – up to 5 years jail + fine).
 Supporting Laws:
 IPC Sections:
 420 (Cheating) – Fraudulent use of funds.
 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) – If multiple entities involved.
 409 (Criminal Breach of Trust) – Diversion of funds.

title: The Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Amendment Act, 2020
description:
The FCRA Amendment Act, 2020 introduced stricter regulations on foreign funding to NGOs, including prohibiting sub-granting, mandatory Aadhaar for office bearers, and stricter fund utilization norms. Violations can lead to cancellation of registration, freezing of funds, and criminal prosecution.Primary Sections (FCRA 2020 Amendments):
 Section 7 (Prohibition on Sub-Granting) – NGOs cannot transfer foreign funds to other entities.
 Section 12A (Mandatory Aadhaar for Office Bearers) – Non-compliance leads to rejection/cancellation.
 Section 17 (SBI Designated FCRA Account) – Funds must be received only in the specified SBI account.
 Section 35 (False Declarations) – Punishable with 6 months imprisonment/fine.
 Section 37 (Misuse of Funds) – Punishable with up to 5 years imprisonment + fine.
 Supporting Laws:
 IPC Sections:
 420 (Cheating) – Fraudulent use of funds.
 120B (Criminal Conspiracy) – If multiple entities involved.
 409 (Criminal Breach of Trust) – Diversion of funds.

title: The Foreigners Act, 1946.
description:
The Foreigners Act, 1946 regulates the entry, stay, and departure of foreigners in India. Violations include illegal entry, overstaying visas, non-compliance with registration requirements, or engaging in prohibited activities. The Act empowers authorities to detain, deport, and prosecute offenders.Primary Sections:
 Section 3 (Power to regulate foreigners)
 Section 14 (Penalty for contravention - up to 5 years imprisonment + fine)
 Section 14A (Penalty for using fake documents)
 Section 14B (Penalty for overstaying)
 Supporting Laws:
 IPC Sections:
 420 (Cheating) - For fake documents
 468 (Forgery) - Forged visas/passports
 471 (Using forged documents as genuine)
 Passport (Entry into India) Act, 1920

title: The Gazette of India Notification (FCRA)
description:
The Gazette of India notifications regarding FCRA typically:
 Announce amendments to FCRA rules
 Specify new compliance requirements
 List organizations banned from receiving foreign funds
 Declare suspensions/cancellations of FCRA registrationsAn FIR can be filed when:
 Operating after cancellation (continuing activities despite MHA's gazette notification of cancellation)
 Violating new rules announced via gazette (e.g., foreign funding restrictions)
 Defying suspension orders published in the gazette

title: The Passport (Entry into India) Act, 1920.
description:
The Passport (Entry into India) Act, 1920 is an Indian law that regulates the entry of individuals into India by requiring them to possess valid travel documents (passports). The Act empowers authorities to:
 Restrict entry of individuals without valid passports.
 Detain or deport unauthorized entrants.
 Prescribe penalties for violations (fines, imprisonment, or both).4. Legal Consequences section 3: Penalty for entering without a passport (up to 3 months imprisonment or fine).
 Section 4: Power to arrest without a warrant if a person enters illegally.
 Section 5: Authorities can deport violators.

title: The Registration of Foreigners Act, 1939.
description:
The Registration of Foreigners Act, 1939 is an Indian law that mandates the registration and monitoring of foreigners staying in India for a specified period. It requires foreigners to:
 Register with authorities (if staying beyond a certain period).
 Report changes in address/occupation.
 Surrender registration certificates upon departure.
 The Act empowers the government to track foreign nationals, enforce compliance, and take legal action against violators.4. Legal Consequences
 Section 3: Empowers authorities to issue registration requirements.
 Section 5: Penalty for non-compliance (imprisonment up to 6 months and/or fine).
 Section 6: Police can arrest without a warrant if a foreigner violates the Act.
 Section 7: Foreigners may be deported for violations.

title: The Citizenship (Amendment) Ordinance, 2015.
description:
The Citizenship (Amendment) Ordinance, 2015 was a temporary executive order (later replaced by the Citizenship (Amendment) Act, 2019) that aimed to amend the Citizenship Act, 1955. Its key provisions included:
 Fast-tracking citizenship for persecuted minorities (Hindus, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains, Parsis, Christians) from Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Bangladesh who entered India before December 31, 2014.
 Exemption from prosecution under the Foreigners Act, 1946 and Passport (Entry into India) Act, 1920 for eligible migrants.
 Reduction in residency requirement from 11 years to 6 years for naturalization.4. Legal Consequences for Fraudulent Claims
 Section 12, Citizenship Act, 1955 – Up to 5 years imprisonment + fine for false representation.
 IPC Sections 420/468/471 – Punishment for cheating & forgery (up to 7 years).
 Deportation – If the person is found ineligible.

title: The Citizenship Rules, 2009.
description:
The Citizenship Rules, 2009 were framed under the Citizenship Act, 1955 to streamline procedures for:
 Acquisition of Indian citizenship (by birth, descent, registration, naturalization).
 Renunciation & termination of citizenship.
 Overseas Citizenship of India (OCI) registration.
 Documentation & verification processes.
 These rules do not create new offences but prescribe administrative procedures for citizenship matters.5. Legal Consequence 
  sec. 12, Citizenship Act – Up to 5 years jail + fine for false representation.
 IPC 420 – Cheating (up to 7 years).
 IPC 465/468 – Forgery (up to 2-7 years).
 Blacklisting – Ban on future citizenship/OCI applications.

title: The Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Rules, 2011.
description:
The Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Rules, 2011 (FCRR, 2011) were framed under the Foreign Contribution (Regulation) Act, 2010 (FCRA) to regulate:
 Receipt of foreign funds by NGOs, associations, and individuals in India.
 Usage restrictions (e.g., no political donations, no speculative activities).
 Compliance requirements (registration, reporting, bank account mandates).
 These rules operationalize the FCRA, 2010 and prescribe procedures for approvals, audits, and penalties.An FIR can be filed if an entity/person:
 Receives foreign funds without FCRA registration (Sec. 11, FCRA).
 Diverts funds for prohibited purposes (e.g., political campaigns, religious conversions).
 Fakes utilization certificates (Rule 17).
 Operates multiple bank accounts (violating Rule 15).
 Submits forged audit reports (Rule 17(4)).

title: The Passport (Entry into India) Rules, 1920.
description:
The Passport (Entry into India) Rules, 1920 were framed under the Passport (Entry into India) Act, 1920 to regulate:
 Entry of foreigners into India by mandating valid travel documents (passport/visa).
 Conditions for entry (e.g., valid visa, no threat to security).
 Power to refuse entry and deport illegal entrants.
 These rules operationalize the 1920 Act and prescribe procedures for immigration checks, detentions, and penalties.Enters India without a valid passport/visa (Sec. 3, Act).
 Overstays beyond visa validity (Rule 4).
 Uses fake travel documents (IPC 420/468).
 Violates visa conditions (e.g., works on a tourist visa).
 Refuses to comply with immigration orders (Sec. 5, Act).

title: The Registration of Foreigners Rules, 1992.
description:
The Registration of Foreigners Rules, 1992 were framed under The Registration of Foreigners Act, 1939 to:
 Track foreign nationals staying in India beyond a specified period.
 Mandate registration with local Foreigners Registration Offices (FROs/FRROs).
 Regulate movement (e.g., reporting address changes, surrendering certificates upon exit).
 These rules operationalize the 1939 Act and prescribe forms, timelines, and compliance procedures.An FIR can be initiated if a foreigner:
 Fails to register with FRRO/FRO within 14 days of arrival (Rule 5).
 Does not report address changes within 24 hours (Rule 7).
 Overstays beyond visa/registration validity.
 Submits fake documents (e.g., forged rental agreements for registration).
 Refuses inspection by authorities (Rule 10).

title: Amendment to the Enemy Property Act, 1968 (No. 34 of 1968) as amended in 1977 and issue of ordinance to give effect to the proposed new amendments.
description:
The Enemy Property Act, 1968 (amended in 1977, 2010, and 2017) governs properties left behind by individuals who migrated to Pakistan/China during wars (1947, 1965, 1971). Key amendments include:
 1977 Amendment: Clarified that legal heirs of enemies cannot claim property in India.
 2017 Amendment: Made the Custodian’s control permanent (no reversion even after enemy’s death).
 Ordinance (2024): Proposed further tightening of rules (if applicable).
 Objective: Prevent unauthorized transfer/sale of enemy properties and safeguard national interests.Enemy Property Act:
 Sec. 6: Eviction + 3 years imprisonment.
 Sec. 8: Penalty for obstructing Custodian.
 IPC:
 Forgery (467): Life imprisonment.
 Cheating (420): 7 years + fine.
 Property reverts to Custodian.

title: Recruitment Rules for the post of Assistant Custodian of Enemy Property for India.
description:
The Recruitment Rules (RRs) for the post of Assistant Custodian of Enemy Property for India are framed under:
 Article 309 of the Indian Constitution (Civil Service Rules).
 Enemy Property Act, 1968 (as amended in 2017).
 Department of Personnel & Training (DoPT) Guidelines.
 Objective: Govern the qualifications, selection process, and duties of officers managing enemy properties under the Custodian of Enemy Property for India (CEPI).5. Legal Consequences
 IPC Charges:
 Cheating (420): Up to 7 years imprisonment.
 Forgery (468): Up to 7 years + fine.
 PCA, 1988:
 Bribery (Sec. 7): 3–7 years imprisonment.
 Departmental Action:
 Dismissal from service (Rule 14, CCS Rules).

title: Recruitment Rules for the post of Custodian of Enemy Property for India.
description:
The Recruitment Rules for Custodian of Enemy Property are framed under:
 Article 309 of the Constitution (Civil Services)
 Enemy Property Act, 1968 (as amended in 2017)
 DoPT (Department of Personnel & Training) guidelineshile Recruitment Rules are administrative, FIRs can be filed for:
 Fraudulent Appointments (IPC Sections 420, 465, 471)
 Corruption in Selection (Prevention of Corruption Act)
 Misuse of Official Position (CCS Conduct Rules)

title: Recruitment Rules for the post of Deputy Custodian of Enemy Property for India.
description:
The Recruitment Rules (RRs) for Deputy Custodian of Enemy Property for India are framed under:
 Article 309 of the Indian Constitution (Civil Services Rules).
 Enemy Property Act, 1968 (as amended in 2017).
 Department of Personnel & Training (DoPT) Guidelines.An FIR can be filed for:
 Recruitment Fraud:
 Fake degrees/experience certificates (IPC 468).
 Bribery in selection (PCA, Sec. 7).
 Misconduct in Office:
 Unauthorized sale/lease of enemy property (IPC 409).
 Tampering with records (IPC 477A).
 Corruption:
 Demanding bribes to release properties (PCA, Sec. 7).

title: The Enemy Property (Amendment and Validation) Fourth ordinance, 2016.
description:
1. Legislative Context
 Parent Law: Enemy Property Act, 1968
 Trigger: Pending litigation on enemy property disputes
 Key Feature: Retrospective application (validates actions since 1968)
 Constitutional Basis: Article 123 (President's ordinance power)3. FIR Triggering Scenarios
 A. For Property Violations
 Unauthorized Occupation (Sec. 6)
 Punishment: 3 yrs imprisonment + fine
 FIR Basis: IPC 447 (Criminal trespass) + EPA provisions
 Document Forgery (for false claims)
 Relevant Sections: IPC 465/467/471
 Case Example: 2018 Lucknow case where fake wills were used
 B. For Official Misconduct
 Custodian's Collusion
 Applicable Laws: Prevention of Corruption Act + IPC 120B
 Evidence Needed: Money trail, recorded conversations

title: The Enemy Property (Amendment and Validation) third ordinance, 2016.
description:
Promulgated: May 28, 2016 (replacing Jan 2016 ordinance)
 Objective:
 Close loopholes in enemy property management
 Retrospectively validate all Custodian actions since 1968
 Block legal challenges by heirs of enemies
 Key Trigger:
 2010 SC ruling in Kaiser-i-Hind case creating ambiguity
 Large-scale litigation by heirs of migrants to PakistanA. For Civilians
 Illegal Occupation (Sec. 6)
 Punishment: 3 yrs + fine
 FIR Sections: EPA + IPC 441 (Criminal trespass)
 Fraudulent Claims
 Example: Forged inheritance documents
 Charges: IPC 467 (Forgery) + 420 (Cheating)

title: The Enemy Property Act, 1968.
description:
1. Legislative Background
 Enacted: September 2, 1968
 Constitutional Basis: Entry 10, List I (Union List) of 7th Schedule
 Amendments: 2017 (major overhaul), with retrospective effect from 1968
 Purpose: Manage properties of individuals who migrated to Pakistan/China during:
 1947 Partition
 1965 Indo-Pak War
 1971 Bangladesh Liberation WarGrounds for FIR:
Unauthorized Occupation (Section 6)
 Applicable IPC Sections: 441 (Criminal trespass), 447 (Punishment for trespass)
 Document Forgery
 IPC Sections: 465, 467, 471
 Corruption in Management

title: Chief Commissioner Gurdwara Election Appointment Rules,2014.
description:
Governing Law: Sikh Gurdwaras Act, 1925 (as amended)
 Authority: Ministry of Minority Affairs (Govt. of India)
 Purpose: Regulate appointment process for Chief Commissioner overseeing SGPC electionsPotential Offences:
 Election Fraud (Rule 15)
 IPC 171C (Undue influence)
 IPC 420 (Cheating)
 Corrupt Practices
 Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988 (Sec. 7)
 Document Forgery (Rule 12)
 IPC 468 (Forgery for cheating)

title: Notification for commencement of the Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU) Act, 2020
description:
The Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU) Act, 2020 was enacted to establish the Rashtriya Raksha University as an institution of national importance for education, training, and research in policing, internal security, and related areas. The commencement of this Act was officially notified by the Government of India.Relevant Sections for FIR (if applicable)
 If the FIR pertains to unauthorized use of university property, fraud, impersonation, or any criminal activity under the Act, reference should be made to:
 Indian Penal Code (IPC) (e.g., Sec 420 – Cheating, Sec 468 – Forgery, etc.)
 RRU Act provisions (if any specific penal clauses exist)
 If it involves cybercrime or data theft related to RRU, IT Act, 2000 (Sec 66) may apply.

title: Official Secrets Acts, 1923.
description:
The Official Secrets Act (OSA), 1923 is an anti-espionage and secrecy law in India that penalizes spying, unauthorized disclosure of classified information, and activities threatening national security.1. Offenses Under OSA, 1923
 Section 3: Spying or sharing sensitive information that benefits an enemy.
 Section 5: Unauthorized access, retention, or disclosure of official secret s.
 Section 7: Attempting or abetting offenses under the Act.
 Section 11: Harboring spies or suspects.

title: THE ARMS (AMENDMENT) ACT, 2019
description:
The Arms (Amendment) Act, 2019 amended the Arms Act, 1959 to impose stricter penalties on illegal possession, manufacturing, and trafficking of firearms in India. Key changes include reduced firearm license quotas, harsher punishments, and new offenses related to organized crime and terrorism.Section Offense Punishment
 Section 3 (Amended) Unlicensed possession of firearmsUp to 7 years (earlier 3 years) + Fine
 Section 7 (New) Possession of prohibited arms (e.g., AK-47, sniper rifles) 10 years to life imprisonment + Fine
 Section 25(1AA) (New) Manufacturing/selling illegal firearms Minimum 10 years, extendable to life + Fine
 Section 27 (Amended) Using arms in organized crime/terrorism Life imprisonment or death
 Section 29 (New) Punishment for snatching firearms (e.g., from police/military) 10 years to life + Fine

title: The Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1961.
description:
The Criminal Law Amendment Act, 1961 is a preventive detention and confiscation law aimed at curbing organized crime, black-marketing, and anti-social activities by allowing the forfeiture of illegally acquired property and imposing stricter penalties.Section Offense Punishment
 Section 3 Unlawful possession of property acquired through crime Confiscation + Fine
 Section 4 Harboring offenders (e.g., gangsters, smugglers) Up to 3 years imprisonment + Fine
 Section 5 Attempting to conceal illegally acquired property Up to 2 years imprisonment + Fine
 Section 6 Abetting or assisting in retaining illegal gains Same as principal offender
 Section 7 Failure to comply with forfeiture orders Additional penalty + Imprisonment

title: The Criminal Law(Amendment) Act, 2013.
description:
The Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2013 was enacted following the Nirbhaya case to strengthen laws related to sexual offenses against women. It introduced stricter punishments, new offenses, and procedural reforms in the Indian Penal Code (IPC), Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC), and Indian Evidence Act.Section 376 (IPC) – Rape
 Minimum 7 years imprisonment, extendable to life term or death in extreme cases.
 Gang rape (Section 376D) mandates minimum 20 years to life imprisonment.
 Section 354 (IPC) – Sexual Harassment
 Punishment increased to 1-3 years imprisonment.
 Section 354A (IPC) – Sexual Harassment (New)
 Covers unwelcome physical contact, demand for sexual favors, etc.
 Section 354B (IPC) – Assault with intent to disrobe
 Punishment: 3-7 years imprisonment.
 Section 354C (IPC) – Voyeurism
 Punishment: 1-3 years (first offense), up to 7 years (repeat offense).
 Section 354D (IPC) – Stalking
 Punishment: 3-5 years imprisonment.
 Section 166A (IPC) – Public Servant’s failure to record FIR
 Punishment: 6 months-2 years imprisonment.
 Section 376A (IPC) – Causing death or vegetative state during rape
 Punishment: 20 years to life or death.

title: The Explosive Substances Act, 1908.
description:
The Explosive Substances Act, 1908 is a stringent anti-terrorism law that criminalizes the manufacture, possession, use, and distribution of explosives for unlawful purposes. It is often invoked in cases of terrorism, sabotage, and organized violence.
 Key Provisions Relevant for FIR Generation
 1. Major Offenses Under the Explosive Substances Act, 1908 
 section 3 – Causing Explosion Likely to Endanger Life or Property
 Punishment: Life imprisonment or 10 years + fine.
 Applies if an explosion causes or risks harm to people/property.
 Section 4 – Attempt to Cause Explosion or Making/Keeping Explosives
 Punishment: Up to 10 years + fine.
 Covers possession, manufacturing, or transporting explosives unlawfully.
 Section 5 – Possession of Explosives Under Suspicious Circumstances
 Punishment: Up to 10 years + fine.
 If a person cannot justify possession, they are presumed guilty.
 Section 6 – Abetment & Conspiracy
 Punishment: Same as the principal offense (life or 10 years).
 Section 9(B) – Illegal Storage/Transport of Explosives (Added Later)
 Punishment: Up to 2 years + fine.

title: The National Security Act, 1980.
description:
The National Security Act (NSA), 1980 is a preventive detention law that allows the government to detain individuals to prevent threats to national security, public order, or essential services. Unlike punitive laws, it focuses on preventing harm rather than prosecuting past crimes.
 Key Provisions Relevant for Detention & Legal Process
 1. Grounds for Detention Under NSA
 Preventing threats to:
 National security (espionage, terrorism, sabotage).
 Public order (communal riots, large-scale violence).
 Maintenance of essential services (strikes disrupting fuel, power, transport).
 Foreign nationals acting against India’s defense/security.

title: The National Security Act,1980-with all latest amendments.
description:
1. Overview & Legal Definition
 The National Security Act (NSA), 1980 is a preventive detention law empowering central/state governments to detain individuals to:
  Prevent threats to national security, public order, or essential services
  Maintain public peace during communal tensions or riots
  Control anti-national activities (espionage, terrorism, fake currency circulation)A) Who Can Be Detained?
 Terror suspects (even if insufficient evidence for UAPA conviction)
 Organized rioters (preventing communal violence)
 Cyber criminals spreading disinformation threatening national security (2023 Amendment)
 Foreign nationals involved in espionage/anti-India activities

title: The Official Secrets Act, 1923.
description:
1. Overview & Purpose
 The Official Secrets Act (OSA), 1923 is India's primary anti-espionage law designed to:
 Protect state secrets and national security
 Prevent unauthorized disclosures of sensitive information
 Combat spying and potential threats to national interests
 2. Key Provisions (Updated Interpretation)
 Section 3: Spying & Wrongful Communication
 Applies to any person who obtains/communicates information that could:
 Benefit enemy states
 Endanger national security
 Affect sovereignty/integrity of India
 Punishment: 3 to 14 years imprisonment

title: The Punjab Special Powers (Press) Act, 1956.
description:
The Punjab Special Powers (Press) Act, 1956 is a state-specific legislation enacted to regulate the printing presses and newspapers in Punjab. It grants the state government special powers to control and censor press content deemed prejudicial to public order, security, or morality.(B) Penalties & Offenses
 Publishing Without License: Punishable with fines and/or imprisonment.
 Violating Government Orders: Printing banned material can lead to press seizure and criminal charges.
 Sedition & Defamation: The Act overlaps with IPC Sections 124A (sedition) and 499 (defamation).

title: The Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU) Act, 2020
description:
The Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU) Act, 2020 established RRU as an Institution of National Importance (INI) under the Ministry of Home Affairs. It aims to:While RRU itself is an academic body, certain offenses may attract legal action:
 (A) Unauthorized Access to RRU Facilities
 Potential Charges:
 IPC Section 447 (Criminal Trespass)
 Official Secrets Act (if sensitive data is breached)
 (B) Fake Certificates/Impersonation
 FIR Under:
 IPC Section 420 (Cheating)
 IPC Section 468 (Forgery)
 (C) Data Theft/Cyber Intrusion
 Applicable Laws:
 IT Act, 2000 (Section 66 – Hacking)
 UAPA (if linked to terrorism)
 ✔ Provide education, training, and research in policing, internal security, and cyber security.
 ✔ Develop specialized programs for law enforcement, defense, and intelligence agencies.
 ✔ Promote collaboration with international security institutions.

title: The Rashtriya Raksha University Act, 2020
description:
Enacted to establish Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU) as an Institution of National Importance
 Primary focus: National security education, training and research
 Rebrands the former Raksha Shakti University (Gujarat) with expanded mandate

title: The Sikh Gurudwaras (Amendment) Act , 2016.
description:
Amendment to: The Sikh Gurdwaras Act, 1925 (primary legislation governing Sikh shrines in India)
 Enacted by: Parliament of India
 Key Purpose: Streamline administration of Sikh shrines and resolve electoral disputes in SGPC elections

title: The Sikh Gurudwaras Act, 1925.
description:
Enacted: 1925 by British India legislature
 Primary Purpose: To govern and manage Sikh shrines (Gurdwaras) in Punjab
 Replaced: The earlier Sikh Gurdwaras and Shrines Act, 1922
 Trigger: Akali Movement's struggle to free Gurdwaras from corrupt mahants (priestly class)

title: The Special Protecting Group (Amendment) Act, 2019
description:
SPG's Mandate: Provides proximate security to the Prime Minister, former Prime Ministers, and their immediate family members (restricted to a period of 5 years after leaving office).
 Exclusion Clause: No automatic protection for former PMs or family members beyond the stipulated period unless a threat assessment warrants it.
 Unauthorized Access/Possession: Unlawful use of SPG insignia, uniforms, or misrepresentation as an SPG member is punishable.
 Penal Provisions: Violations (e.g., impersonation, obstruction of duty) may attract imprisonment and/or fines under Sections 14 & 15 of the SPG Act.
 FIR Relevance:
 If an offense involves impersonation, security breach, or unauthorized access related to SPG-protected individuals, reference this Act in the FIR along with relevant IPC sections (e.g., 419, 468, 471).

title: The Young Persons (Harmful Publications) Act, 1956.
description:
Prevents the dissemination of harmful publications (e.g., comics, books, magazines) that could corrupt young persons (under 20 years) by glorifying crime, violence, or immoral acts.
 Offenses Under the Act (Relevant for FIR):
 Printing, Producing, or Publishing Harmful Material – Any content likely to incite violence, crime, or delinquency among youth.
 Selling, Distributing, or Circulating – Unlawful supply of such publications to minors.
 Possession for Sale – Keeping harmful publications with intent to distribute.
 Penalties (Section 3):
 First Offense: Up to 6 months imprisonment and/or fine.
 Subsequent Offenses: Up to 1 year imprisonment and/or fine.

title: Gazette Notification for RRU Rules
description: The Rashtriya Raksha University (RRU) Rules are notified by the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) or Government of India via Official Gazette to govern the functioning, administration, and legal framework of RRU (a central university under the Rashtriya Raksha University Act, 2020).

title: Sikh Gurdwaras Board Election Rules, 1959
description:
These rules govern the conduct of elections for the Shiromani Gurdwara Parbandhak Committee (SGPC) and other Sikh religious bodies under the Sikh Gurdwaras Act, 1925.Electoral Offenses
 Unlawful Candidacy: Filing nomination fraudulently (e.g., fake affidavits, ineligibility).
 Booth Capturing/Intimidation: Preventing voters from casting ballots.
 Bribery/Corruption: Offering money/benefits for votes.
 False Declarations: Misrepresentation in election documents.
 Possible IPC Sections:
 171F (Undue influence at elections)
 171G (False statement in connection with elections)
 186 (Obstructing public servant in duty)
 IPC 420 (Cheating) if forgery involved

title: The Arms Rules, 1962.
description:
The Arms Rules, 1962, framed under the Arms Act, 1959, regulate the licensing, possession, sale, and manufacture of firearms and ammunition in India. Violations can lead to criminal charges.1. Illegal Possession of Firearms/Ammunition
 Section 25(1)(a) of Arms Act: Possession without a valid license.
 IPC Section 411: Dishonestly receiving stolen arms.
 Punishment: Up to 7 years imprisonment + fine.
 2. Unauthorized Sale/Manufacture of Arms
 section 25(1AA) of Arms Act: Illegal sale/procurement.
 Section 27(3): Using arms for unlawful violence.
 Punishment: Up to life imprisonment (if used in violent crime).
 3. Fake License or Tampering with Records
 Section 30 of Arms Act: Fraudulent license application.
 IPC 420 (Cheating) + 468 (Forgery) if fake documents used.
 4. Violation of License Conditions
 Section 5 of Arms Act: Using licensed arms for unpermitted purposes.
 Example: Sporting gun used for intimidation.
 5. Illegal Import/Export of Arms
 Section 25(1D) of Arms Act: Smuggling firearms across borders.
 Customs Act + COFEPOSA may apply.

title: The Acquisition of Certain Area at Ayodhya Act, 1993.
description:
This Act facilitated the acquisition of land in Ayodhya related to the Ram Janmabhoomi-Babri Masjid dispute to maintain public order and eventual settlement.Relevant Offenses for FIR (If Applicable)
 1. Unauthorized Entry/Occupation of Acquired Land
 Section 7(1): Entering or occupying the acquired area (67.703 acres) without permission is punishable.
 Punishment: Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine.
 2. Obstruction of Government Duties
 Section 7(2): Preventing authorities from taking possession of the land.
 IPC Sections 186 (Obstructing public servant) + 353 (Assault on public servant) may apply.
 3. Damage/Destruction of Property
 Section 7(3): Unlawful demolition, construction, or damage to structures on acquired land.
 IPC Sections 425 (Mischief) + 447 (Criminal trespass) may apply.
 4. Violation of Central Government’s Authority
 The Act vests the land solely with the Central Government (Section 3). Any unauthorized claim or transfer is void.

title: The Place of Worship (Special Provisions) Act, 1991.
description:
This secularism-protection law freezes the religious character of all places of worship as it existed on August 15, 1947, and prohibits their conversion. Exemption: Ram Janmabhoomi-Babri Masjid dispute (settled separately).This secularism-protection law freezes the religious character of all places of worship as it existed on August 15, 1947, and prohibits their conversion. Exemption: Ram Janmabhoomi-Babri Masjid dispute (settled separately).Relevant Offenses for FIR (Non-Bailable in Many Cases)
 1. Conversion/Alteration of a Place of Worship
 Section 3: No person shall convert:
 A place of worship of any religion into one of another religion.
 The religious identity of any place of worship from its 1948 status.
 Punishment: Up to 3 years imprisonment + fine.
 2. Disturbing Religious Assemblies
 Section 4(1): No lawsuits to reclaim/change a place of worship’s 1948 status.
 Section 4(2): All pending cases (except Ram Janmabhoomi) stand abated.
 IPC 295A (Deliberate insult to religion) may apply if violence occurs.
 3. Inciting Communal Tensions
 Section 6: Any attempt to forcefully claim/occupy a place of worship is punishable.
 IPC 153A (Promoting enmity between groups) can be added.

title: The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
description:
This Act establishes the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) and State Human Rights Commissions (SHRCs) to investigate violations of human rights by public servants or systemic failures.1. Human Rights Violations by Public Servants
 Section 12(a): NHRC/SHRC can probe:
 Police brutality (custodial torture, fake encounters).
 Illegal detention (beyond 24 hrs without magistrate nod).
 Atrocities against SC/STs (under SC/ST Act).
 Failure to protect rights (e.g., lynching, child labor).
 IPC Sections Applicable:
 330 (Voluntarily causing hurt to extract confession).
 342 (Wrongful confinement).
 302 (Murder) + 201 (Destruction of evidence) in custodial deaths.

title: Religious Institutions (Prevention of Misuse) Act, 1988
description:
Purpose: Prevents misuse of religious sites for political activities, storing arms, or illegal assemblies.Key Offenses (FIR Applicable)
 Using Religious Places for Non-Religious Activities
 Section 3: Bans use of temples/mosques/churches/etc. for:
 Political meetings/propaganda.
 Storing weapons/explosives (*IPC 120B/121A if linked to conspiracy*).
 Harboring criminals (IPC 212).
 Unauthorized Financial Collections
 Section 4: Prohibits fundraising without legal sanction (linked to IPC 420 if fraudulent).
 Violation by Trustees/Managers
 Section 5: Penalty for custodians allowing illegal use (up to 5 years jail + fine).

title: Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2018
description:
The Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2018 introduced key changes to the Indian Penal Code (IPC), Indian Evidence Act, and Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC) concerning sexual offenses, particularly against women and children. Below are the essential provisions relevant for FIR generation:
 Key Amendments for FIR Purposes:
 IPC Section 376AB – Rape of a woman under 12 years of age:
 Punishment: Minimum 20 years imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment or death.
 FIR must be filed under this section if the victim is a girl below 12.
 IPC Section 376DA – Gang rape of a woman under 16 years of age:
 Punishment: Life imprisonment or death.
 Applicable if multiple offenders are involved.
 IPC Section 376DB – Gang rape of a woman under 12 years of age:
 Punishment: Life imprisonment or death.
 FIR must mention this section if the victim is below 12 and multiple perpetrators are involved.
 IPC Section 376(3) – Rape resulting in death or persistent vegetative state:
 Punishment: Minimum 20 years to life imprisonment or death.
 FIR should include this if the victim dies or is left in a vegetative state.
 Time-bound Investigation & Trial:
 Investigation must be completed within 2 months (as per amended CrPC provisions).
 Trial should ideally conclude within 6 months.
 No Anticipatory Bail (Section 438 CrPC Barred):
 If the accused is charged under Sections 376AB, 376DA, or 376DB, anticipatory bail is not allowed.

title: The Code of Criminal Procedure (Amendment) Act, 2005.
description:
The Code of Criminal Procedure (Amendment) Act, 2005 introduced key procedural changes in criminal investigations and trials. Below are the essential provisions relevant for FIR generation and police action:
 Key Amendments for FIR Purposes:
 Section 41A (Notice before Arrest) 
 Police must issue a notice (under Section 41A CrPC) to an accused appearing in a cognizable offense punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment.
 Arrest should be avoided if the accused complies with the notice.
 FIR must record reasons if arrest is still made.
 Section 41B (Procedure for Arrest) –
 Police must prepare a memorandum of arrest, attested by at least one witness (family member or respectable person).
 The arrested person has the right to inform a relative/friend.
 Section 50A (Obligation to Inform Arrest) –
 Police must inform a relative/friend of the arrestee about:
 The arrest,
 Custody location,
 Offense details.
 Failure to comply may affect investigation validity.
 Section 54 (Medical Examination of Arrested Person) –
 Arrested person has a right to medical examination to record injuries (if any).
 FIR must mention if medical examination was conducted.
 Section 173 (Time-bound Investigation) –
 Investigation in offenses punishable with up to 10 years must be completed within 60 days (else bail may be granted).
 Chargesheet must be filed within this period.
 Section 436A (Default Bail) –
 If trial is not completed within half the maximum sentence period, accused becomes eligible for default bail.

title: The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973.
description:
The CrPC, 1973 governs criminal procedure in India. Below are the key sections relevant for FIR registration, investigation, and arrest procedures that must be followed by police when filing an FIR:(A) Arrest Provisions
 Section 41 CrPC – When Arrest Can Be Made
 Police can arrest without a warrant in cognizable cases if:
 Accused committed a cognizable offense.
 There is a reasonable suspicion.
 Arrest is necessary to prevent further crime, evidence tampering, or fleeing.
 Section 41A CrPC – Notice Before Arrest (For Offenses Punishable Up to 7 Years)
 Police must issue a notice (instead of arrest) if the offense carries ≤7 years imprisonment.
 Arrest only if accused fails to comply with the notice.
 Section 46 CrPC – Manner of Arrest
 No unnecessary restraint; no woman to be arrested after sunset unless exceptional (with Magistrate’s written permission).
 Section 50 CrPC – Right to Know Grounds of Arrest
 Arrested person must be informed of charges immediately.
 Section 50A CrPC – Duty to Inform Relative/Friend
 Police must notify a relative/friend about the arrest.
 Section 54 CrPC – Medical Examination of Arrested Person
 Accused has a right to medical examination to record injuries (if any).
 (B) Investigation & Chargesheet Filing
 Section 156 CrPC – Police Power to Investigate
 Police can investigate without Magistrate’s order in cognizable cases.
 Section 157 CrPC – Procedure for Investigation
 Police must send FIR to Magistrate and proceed to the crime scene.
 If no sufficient grounds, police can close the case with Magistrate’s approval.
 Section 167 CrPC – Maximum Police Custody & Judicial Remand
 Police Custody: Max 15 days (total).
 Judicial Custody: Up to 60/90 days (depending on offense severity).
 If chargesheet is not filed within 90 days (for serious offenses), accused gets default bail (Section 167(2)) 
 Section 173 CrPC – Filing Chargesheet
 Investigation must be completed within 60-90 days (depending on offense).
 Final report (chargesheet/closure report) must be submitted to Magistrate.
 4. Victim & Witness Protection 
 Section 160 CrPC – Witness Examination
 No male below 15 years or woman shall be called to the police station (they can be examined at their residence).
 Section 161 CrPC – Recording Witness Statements
 Police must record statements accurately (can be used in trial).

title: The Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952.
description:
1. Establishment of a Commission (Section 3)
 Central/State Government can appoint a Commission to inquire into:
 Any matter of public importance (e.g., riots, scandals, disasters).
 Any violation of constitutional rights or misuse of authority.
 Commission has powers of a Civil Court (summoning witnesses, demanding documents, etc.).
 Relevance for FIRs: 
 If a Commission uncovers criminal offenses (e.g., corruption, murder, conspiracy), it can recommend FIR registration.
 Police must act on such recommendations if they disclose cognizable offenses (Section 154 CrPC).

title: The Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2005.
description:
The Criminal Law (Amendment) Act, 2005 introduced important changes to criminal laws in India, particularly focusing on sexual offenses, custodial crimes, and witness protection. Below are the key provisions relevant for FIR registration and police procedures:
 1. Key Amendments Relevant for FIRs
 A) Sexual Offenses (IPC Amendments)
 Section 376(2)(g) IPC (Gang Rape)
 Made punishment more stringent (minimum 10 years, extendable to life imprisonment).
 FIR must clearly mention if multiple accused involved.
 Section 376A IPC (Rape Resulting in Death/Persistent Vegetative State)
 Introduced death penalty for rape causing death or permanent vegetative state.
 FIR must include medical evidence of victim's condition.
 (B) Custodial Crimes (New Sections)
 Section 376B IPC (Sexual Intercourse by Public Servant with Woman in Custody)
 Criminalized sexual exploitation by public servants (police, jail officials, etc.).
 FIR must be registered immediately if such allegations arise.
 Section 376C IPC (Sexual Intercourse by Superintendent of Jail/Remand Home)
 Punishes sexual abuse by authority figures in custody.
 Mandatory FIR even if victim doesn’t complain (suo moto action).

title: The Indian Penal Code Act, 1860.
description:
The Indian Penal Code (1860) forms the backbone of criminal law in India. Below are the key sections most relevant for FIR registration, categorized by offense type for easy reference:
 1. Fundamental FIR-Related Provisions
 A) General Principles
 Section 34 (Common Intention)
 Used when multiple accused act together
 Must be added in FIR if crime involves group participation
 Section 120B (Criminal Conspiracy)
 For pre-planned crimes
 FIR should mention if evidence suggests conspiracy
 B) Against Human Body
 Section Offense FIR Essentials
 299-304 Murder/Culpable Homicide Specify weapon, intention, eyewitnesses
 307Attempt to Murder Mention nature of attack and victim's condition
 323-326 Hurt/Grievous Hurt Medical report required
 354 Outraging Modesty Describe exact acts, preserve CCTV evidence
 C) Property Crimes
 Section Offense FIR Essentials
 378-382 Theft Value of stolen goods, how theft was committed
 32-397 Robbery/Dacoity Weapons used, number of assailants
 406-409 Criminal Breach of Trust Document financial transactions
 D) Sexual Offenses
 Section Offense Special FIR Requirements
 375-376 Rape Record exact time/location, preserve forensic evidence
 376D Gang Rape List all accused, their roles
 354A-D Sexual Harassment Save electronic evidence (messages, emails)
 E) Special Offenses
 Section Offense FIR Action Points
 295A Religious Offenses Record community impact
 498A Dowry Harassment List dowry demands, injury marks
 503 Criminal Intimidation Preserve threat messages

title: The Commissions of Inquiry (Central) Rules,1972.
description: The Commissions of Inquiry (Central) Rules, 1972 govern the procedural aspects of commissions appointed under the Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952. These rules outline how commissions function, including evidence collection, witness examination, and report submission. Below are the key provisions relevant for police, government authorities, and legal professionals involved in commission-related matters.

title: Declaration of the National Liberation Front of Tripura and All Tripura Tiger Force as Unlawful Associations under sub-section(1) of section 3 of the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967-service of show cause notice reg.
description:
The Commissions of Inquiry (Central) Rules, 1972 govern the procedural aspects of commissions appointed under the Commissions of Inquiry Act, 1952. These rules outline how commissions function, including evidence collection, witness examination, and report submission. Below are the key provisions relevant for police, government authorities, and legal professionals involved in commission-related matters.
 1. Key Provisions for Commission Proceedings
 (A) Constitution of Commission (Rule 3)
 The Central Government issues a notification specifying:
 The commission’s purpose and scope of inquiry.
 The chairperson and members (if multi-member).
 The timeframe for submitting the report (unless extended).

title: The Armed Forces (Special Powers) Act, 1958.
description:
The Armed Forces (Special Powers) Act, 1958 (AFSPA) is an emergency legislation that grants special powers to Indian armed forces and central police forces deployed in disturbed areas to maintain public order. It provides:
 Legal Immunity to security forces for actions taken in good faith during operations.
 Extraordinary Powers, including the use of lethal force, arrest without warrant, and search without judicial authorization.
 Limited Judicial Oversight, as prosecution requires prior sanction from the Central Government.

title: The Assam Rifles Act, 2006
description:
The Assam Rifles Act, 2006 is a parliamentary legislation that governs the organization, discipline, powers and duties of the Assam Rifles - a central paramilitary force with dual roles of maintaining internal security and border protection, particularly in Northeast India.
 Core Legal Provisions
 1. Constitutional Status (Section 3)
 Establishes Assam Rifles as an armed force of the Union
 Governed directly by the Ministry of Home Affairs
 Functions under both Army Act and this special legislation

title: The Border Security Force Act, 1968.
description:
The Border Security Force Act, 1968 is a parliamentary legislation that governs the organization, discipline, powers, and duties of the Border Security Force (BSF) – India's primary border-guarding force deployed along international borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh.
 Core Legal Provisions
 1. Constitutional Status (Section 3)
 Establishes the BSF as an armed force of the Union under the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA).
 Personnel are subject to military-style discipline under this Act.
 2. Special Powers Granted (Section 4-5)
 Border Security Duties:
 Prevent cross-border crimes (infiltration, smuggling, trafficking).
 Detain suspects attempting illegal border crossings.
 Arrest & Search Powers (Section 5):
 Arrest without warrant if suspect violates border laws.
 Search premises/seize goods suspected of being smuggled.
 Use necessary force (including firearms) in self-defense or to prevent a felony

title: The Central Industrial Security Act, 1968.
description:
The Central Industrial Security Force Act, 1968 is a parliamentary legislation that governs the organization, duties, powers, and discipline of the Central Industrial Security Force (CISF) - a central armed police force responsible for providing industrial security to critical infrastructure installations across India.
 Core Legal Provisions
 1. Constitutional Status (Section 3)
 Establishes CISF as an armed force of the Union under the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA)
 Personnel are subject to central police force discipline
 2. Special Powers Granted
 Section 4: Security duties at:
 Central Government industrial undertakings
 Critical infrastructure (airports, metro, power plants)
 Public sector undertakings
 Section 5: Powers to:
 Arrest without warrant (within protected premises)
 Search persons/vehicles entering protected areas
 Use reasonable force for security duties

title: The Central Reserve Police Force Act, 1949.
description:
The Central Reserve Police Force Act, 1949 is the primary legislation governing the organization, administration, powers and duties of India's largest central armed police force - the CRPF. This Act establishes the CRPF as a specialized armed police force under the Ministry of Home Affairs for maintaining public order and internal security.
 Core Legal Provisions
 1. Constitutional Status (Section 3)
 Creates CRPF as an armed police force of the Union
 Operates under Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA)
 Personnel subject to central police disciplinary rules
 2. Special Powers Granted
 Section 4: Authorizes deployment for:
 Riot control and crowd management
 Counter-insurgency operations
 Protection of vital installations
 Election security duties
 Section 5: Grants powers to:
 Arrest without warrant during operations
 Use reasonable force including firearms
 Search suspicious persons/vehicles
 3. Legal Immunity (Section 7)
 Protection for acts done in good faith during duty
 Requires Central Government sanction for prosecution
 Does not cover willful misuse of power

title: The Indo Tibetan Border Police Act, 1992.
description:
The Indo-Tibetan Border Police Force Act, 1992 is an Act to provide for the constitution and regulation of the Indo-Tibetan Border Police Force (ITBPF) for ensuring the security of India's borders with Tibet and related matters.
 Key Details Relevant for FIR Generation:
 Jurisdiction – Applies to ITBPF personnel and offenses committed under this Act.
 Offenses – Includes insubordination, desertion, violence, neglect of duty, or any act prejudicial to good order and discipline.
 Authority to Investigate – ITBPF authorities have the power to investigate offenses committed by its members.
 FIR Applicability – If a cognizable offense is committed by an ITBPF member, an FIR can be lodged under relevant sections of this Act along with IPC (if applicable).

title: The National Security Guard Act, 1986.
description:
The National Security Guard (NSG) Act, 1986 provides for the constitution and regulation of an elite counter-terrorism and special operations force in India.
 Relevant Details for FIR Generation:
 Jurisdiction – Applies to NSG personnel and offenses committed under this Act.
 Offenses – Includes disobedience, desertion, misconduct, breach of duty, or any act compromising national security.
 Authority to Investigate – NSG authorities handle internal disciplinary matters, but cognizable offenses may require police/FIR involvement.
 FIR Applicability – If an NSG member commits a cognizable offense (e.g., assault, misuse of authority, or criminal misconduct), an FIR can be lodged under:
 Relevant sections of the NSG Act, 1986 (if applicable)
 Indian Penal Code (IPC) or other laws (e.g., UAPA if terrorism-linked)

title: The Sashastra Seema Bal Act, 2007.
description:
The Sashastra Seema Bal Act, 2007 governs the functioning, discipline, and duties of the SSB, a Central Armed Police Force (CAPF) deployed for border security and internal security operations.
 Relevant Details for FIR Generation:
 Jurisdiction – Applies to SSB personnel for offenses committed under this Act.
 Key Offenses – Includes:
 Disobedience of lawful orders (Sec. 14)
 Desertion or absence without leave (Sec. 15)
 Assault, insubordination, or misconduct (Sec. 16)
 Neglect of duty (Sec. 17)
 Any act prejudicial to discipline/security (Sec. 18)

title: The Assam Rifles Rules, 2010.
description:
The Assam Rifles Rules, 2010 govern the administration, discipline, and operational conduct of the Assam Rifles, a central paramilitary force under the Ministry of Home Affairs.
 Relevant Provisions for FIR Filing
 1. Jurisdiction
 Applies to Assam Rifles personnel for offenses under:
 The Assam Rifles Act, 2006\n Assam Rifles Rules, 2010
 IPC/CrPC (if civilian laws are breached)
 2. Common Offenses (Cognizable & Non-Cognizable)
 Offense Relevant Rule/Section
 Desertion/Absence without leave Rule 14, Assam Rifles Rules
 Disobeying lawful orders Rule 15
 Assaulting superior officer Rule 16
 Neglect of duty Rule 17
 Drunkenness on duty Rule 18
 Corruption/Bribery IPC Sec. 7-12 (PCA Act)
 Misuse of weapons Arms Act, 1959

title: The Border Security Force Rules, 1969.
description:
The Border Security Force Rules, 1969 govern the discipline, duties, and legal procedures for BSF personnel under the BSF Act, 1968.
 Essential Information for FIR Filing
 1. Jurisdiction
 Applies to BSF personnel for offenses under:
 BSF Act, 1968
 BSF Rules, 1969
 IPC/CrPC (if civilian laws are violated)
 2. Common Offenses (Cognizable & Non-Cognizable)
 Offense Relevant Rule/Section
 Desertion/Absence without leave Rule 14, BSF Rules
 Disobeying lawful orders Rule 15
 Assaulting superior officer Rule 16
 Neglect of duty Rule 17
 Drunkenness on duty Rule 18
 Corruption/Bribery IPC Sec. 7-12 (PCA Act)
 Misuse of weapons Arms Act, 1959

title: The Central Industrial Security Rules, 2001.
description:
The Central Industrial Security Force Rules, 2001 govern the discipline, duties, and legal procedures for CISF personnel under the CISF Act, 1968.
 Essential Information for FIR Filing
 1. Jurisdiction
 Applies to CISF personnel for offenses committed under:
 CISF Act, 1968
 CISF Rules, 2001
 IPC/CrPC (if civilian laws are violated)
 2. Common Offenses
 Desertion/Absence without leave (Rule 14, CISF Rules)
 Disobeying lawful orders (Rule 15)
 Assaulting superior officer (Rule 16)
 Neglect of duty (Rule 17)
 Drunkenness on duty (Rule 18)
 Corruption/Bribery (IPC Sec. 7-12, Prevention of Corruption Act)
 Misuse of weapons (Arms Act, 1959)
 Theft/Security breach (IPC Sec. 378/409 if applicable)

title: The Central Reserve Police Force Rules, 1955.
description:
Governs disciplinary and administrative matters for CRPF personnel under the CRPF Act, 1949
 Applies to:
 Active duty CRPF members
 Offenses committed while on duty
 Breaches of CRPF conduct rules
 2. Common Offenses and Corresponding Provisions
 Desertion: Rule 14 (Absence without leave)
 Insubordination: Rule 15 (Disobeying lawful commands)
 Assault: Rule 16 (Violence against superiors/comrades)
 Neglect of Duty: Rule 17 (Security lapses/failure in duty)
 Corruption: Rule 18 + IPC Section 7-13 (PCA Act)
 Weapons Misuse: Rule 19 + Arms Act provisions

title: The Indo Tibetan Border Police Rules, 1994.
description:
The ITBP Rules, 1994 constitute the statutory framework governing the:
 Administration and discipline of ITBP Force members
 Conduct regulations for personnel under the ITBP Act, 1992
 Operational protocols for border security operations
 Judicial mechanisms for disciplinary proceedings
 Legal Basis: Framed under Section 147 of the ITBP Act, 1992 (Act No. 35 of 1992)
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Cognizable Offenses
 Desertion (Rule 14)
 Absence without leave > 21 days
 Attempt to desert border post
 Combat Misconduct (Rule 15)
 Cowardice during operational duty
 Willful surrender of post
 Security Breaches (Rule 16)
 Unauthorized border crossing
 Sensitive information leakage
 Equipment Violations (Rule 17)
 Willful damage to arms/ammunition
 Unauthorized arms transfer

title: The National Security Guard Rules, 1987.
description:
The NSG Rules, 1987 constitute the statutory framework governing:
 Elite counter-terrorism operations under the NSG Act, 1986
 Specialized combat protocols for Black Cat commandos
 Counter-hijacking procedures for aviation security
 VIP protection details (including SPG-coordinated missions)
 Legal Basis: Framed under Section 21 of the NSG Act, 1986 (Act No. 47 of 1986)
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Critical Offenses (Zero-Tolerance)
 Counter-Terrorism Breaches (Rule 14)
 Unauthorized disclosure of:
 Hostage rescue protocols
 Entry-point blueprints
 Commando deployment patterns
 Weapons Security Violations (Rule 15)
 Negligent handling of:
 Counter-sniper systems
 Breaching explosives
 Non-lethal tactical gear
 Operational Compromise (Rule 16)
 Premature disclosure of:
 Live mission coordinates
 Terrorist interrogation techniques
 NSG vehicular camouflage specs

title: The Sashastra Seema Bal Rule, 2009.
description:
The Sashastra Seema Bal Rules, 2009 govern the discipline, duties, and operational conduct of the SSB under the SSB Act, 2007. These rules apply to:
 Border security operations (India-Nepal & India-Bhutan borders)
 Internal security duties (anti-smuggling, counter-insurgency)
 Disciplinary proceedings against SSB personnel
 Legal Basis: Framed under Section 141 of the SSB Act, 2007
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Key Offenses Under SSB Rules
 Desertion/Absence Without Leave (Rule 14)
 Unauthorized absence for more than 21 days
 Attempting to flee while posted in sensitive border areas
 Disobedience of Lawful Orders (Rule 15)
 Refusing deployment orders
 Ignoring operational protocols
 Assault on Superior Officers (Rule 16)
 Physical/violent misconduct against seniors
 Threatening behavior during duty
 Neglect of Duty (Rule 17)
 Failure to prevent smuggling/cross-border crimes
 Sleeping/dereliction while on guard duty
 Misuse of Firearms (Rule 18 + Arms Act)
 Unauthorized firing at borders
 Illegal weapon modifications
 Corruption & Bribery (Rule 19 + IPC Sec. 7-12)
 Taking bribes for allowing illegal crossings
 Fake seizures of smuggled goods

title: The Private Security Agencies (Regulation) Act, 2005.
description:
The Private Security Agencies (Regulation) Act, 2005 (PSARA) governs the licensing, regulation, and conduct of private security agencies and personnel in India. It ensures:
 Proper background checks of security guards
 Training standards for private security providers
 Legal accountability for misconduct
 Enacted by: Parliament of India
 effective from: 15th June 2005
 Administered by: State Governments & Central Licensing Authority
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Key Offenses Under PSARA
 Operating Without License (Section 4)
 Running a security agency without state approval
 Punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year + fine
 Employing Unverified Guards (Section 7)
 Hiring personnel without police verification
 Punishment: Fine up to ₹25,000
 Impersonation/Fake Credentials (Section 10)
 Fake ID cards/uniform misuse
 Punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year + fine
 Security Guard Misconduct (Section 17)
 Theft, assault, or negligence while on duty
 Punishment: IPC charges + license cancellation
 Violating Code of Conduct (Section 18)
 Unauthorized weapons use
 Drunkenness on duty

title: The Census Act, 1948.
description:
The Census Act, 1948 governs the collection, compilation, and publication of demographic data in India. It ensures:
 Mandatory participation in census operations
 Confidentiality of collected data
 Legal penalties for non-compliance or false information
 Administered by: Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India
 Key Authority: Union Ministry of Home Affairs
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Key Offenses Under the Census Act
 Refusal to Provide Information (Section 6)
 Punishment: Fine up to ₹1,000
 Intentionally False Statements (Section 11)
 Punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months + Fine up to ₹1,000
 Unauthorized Disclosure of Data (Section 15)
 Punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months + Fine up to ₹1,000
 Obstructing Census Operations (Section 13)
 Punishment: Fine up to ₹1,000
 Impersonating a Census Officer (Section 14)
 Punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months + Fine up to ₹1,000

title: The Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969.
description:
The Registration of Births and Deaths Act, 1969 mandates:
 Compulsory registration of all births and deaths in India
 Maintenance of proper records by state governments
 Issuance of birth/death certificates
 Penalties for non-compliance or fraudulent registrations
 Administered by: Registrar General of India (RGI) under Ministry of Home Affairs
 Implemented by: State-level Chief Registrars and local registrars
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Key Offenses Under the Act
 Failure to Register (Section 23)
 Not reporting birth/death within 21 days
 Penalty: ₹50 (may vary by state amendments)
 False Information (Section 23)
 Providing incorrect details in registration
 Penalty: ₹50 + potential IPC charges
 Unauthorized Registrations (Section 25)
 Fraudulently registering births/deaths
 Penalty: ₹50 + possible forgery charges
 Tampering with Records (Section 25)
 Altering official birth/death registers
 May attract IPC 468 (forgery) + other charges
 Impersonation (Section 25)
 Posing as registrar/authorized person
 May involve IPC 170 (impersonating official)

title: The Census Rules, 1990.
description:
The Census Rules, 1990 operationalize the Census Act, 1948 by specifying:
 Detailed procedures for census operations
 Duties and powers of census officers
 Data collection and processing protocols
 Confidentiality safeguards for collected information
 Framed under: Section 18 of the Census Act, 1948
 Administered by: Registrar General and Census Commissioner of India
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Key Violations Attracting Penal Action
 Obstructing Census Operations (Rule 6)
 Preventing census officers from performing duties
 Penalty: Fine up to ₹1,000 (Section 13 of Act)
 False Declarations (Rule 8)
 Willfully providing incorrect information
 Penalty: ₹1,000 fine (Section 11 of Act)
 Unauthorized Data Disclosure (Rule 12)
 Breaching confidentiality of census records
 Penalty: 6 months imprisonment + fine (Section 15)
 Impersonation of Officials (Rule 4)
 Posing as census officer without authorization
 Penalty: 6 months imprisonment (Section 14)

title: The Citizenship (Registration of Citizens and Issue of National Identity Cards) Rules, 2003.
description:
The Citizenship (Registration of Citizens and Issue of National Identity Cards) Rules, 2003 provide the framework for:
 Creation of the National Register of Indian Citizens (NRIC)
 Issuance of National Identity Cards
 Verification process for citizenship status
 Documentation requirements for citizenship proof
 Authority: Framed under Section 18 of the Citizenship Act, 1955
 Administered by: Registrar General of Citizen Registration (under MHA)
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Key Offenses Under the Rules
 False Declaration of Citizenship (Rule 4(3))
 Providing fake documents for NRIC registration
 Punishable under: IPC 420 (Cheating), 471 (Using forged docs)
 Impersonation During Registration (Rule 7)
 Pretending to be someone else during verification
 Punishable under: IPC 419 (Cheating by impersonation)
 Tampering with NRIC Records (Rule 10)
 Unauthorized alterations in citizenship records
 Punishable under: IPC 468 (Forgery), 477A (Falsification)
 Obstructing Verification Process (Rule 11)
 Preventing officials from conducting surveys
 Punishable under: IPC 186 (Obstructing public servant)
 Fraudulent Possession of ID Cards (Rule 12)
 Using fake/obtained National Identity Cards
 Punishable under: IPC 465 (Forgery), 471 (Using forged docs)

title: Delhi Municipal Corporation Act 1957.
description:
The Delhi Municipal Corporation Act, 1957 governs:
 Municipal administration in Delhi
 Civic services and urban governance
 Taxation and property regulations
 Public health and sanitation
 Licensing and urban development
 Administered by: Municipal Corporation of Delhi (MCD)
 Key Authorities: Commissioner, Deputy Commissioners, Municipal Officers
 II. FIR-Relevant Provisions
 A. Criminal Offenses Under the Act
 Illegal Construction (Section 332-333)
 Unauthorized building/renovation
 Non-compliance with demolition orders
 Punishment: Fine up to ₹1 lakh + daily penalty
 Property Tax Evasion (Section 150)
 Willful non-payment for 3+ years
 Punishment: Attachment of property + prosecution
 Public Nuisance (Section 388)
 Obstructing streets/footpaths
 Unauthorized street vending
 Punishment: Fine up to ₹5,000
 Sanitation Violations (Section 417)
 Improper waste disposal
 Polluting public water sources
 Punishment: Fine up to ₹50,000
 Corruption by Officials (Section 490)
 Bribery in licensing/approvals
 Punishment: IPC 161 + departmental action

title: Gazette Extraordinary Notification of the Delhi Municipal Corporation(Amendment) Act,2011(Delhi Act 12 of 2011)
description:
An amendment to the Delhi Municipal Corporation Act, 1957, restructuring municipal governance in Delhi.
 Key provisions include:
 Division of Municipal Corporation of Delhi (MCD) into smaller bodies.
 Changes in taxation, property regulations, and administrative powers.
 Violations under this Act may include:
 Illegal construction (Section 347).
 Non-payment of property taxes (Section 150).
 Corruption by municipal officials (Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988).

title: New Delhi Municipal Council Act, 1994.
description:
Governs civic administration in New Delhi’s posh zones (e.g., Lutyens’ Delhi).
 Common Violations:
 Unauthorized Construction (Section 238)
 Building without NDMC approval.
 Property Tax Evasion (Section 61)
 Non-payment or concealment of taxable property.
 Encroachment (Section 322)
 Illegal occupation of public land.
 Corruption by Officials (Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988)
 Bribery in approvals/clearances.

title: The Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu (Merger of Union territories) Act, 2019.
description:
Merged the UTs of Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu into a single UT (w.e.f. 26-Jan-2020).
 Key Provisions & Potential Violations:
 Illegal Land Transfers (Section 4)
 Fraudulent property transactions during/after merger.
 Misuse of Public Funds (Section 7)
 Corruption in allocation of merged UT resources.
 Administrative Fraud (Section 5)
 Fake appointments or backdated orders.
 Violation of Transitional Provisions
 Non-compliance with merger timelines/rules.

title: The Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi Act, 1991.
description:
Defines the powers and functions of the Delhi Legislative Assembly & Council of Ministers.
 Common Violations:
 Misuse of Power (Section 44)
 Unauthorized financial decisions by officials.
 Violation of Administrative Procedures (Section 22)
 Bypassing Lieutenant Governor’s (LG) approval where mandated.
 Corruption in Governance (Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988)
 Bribery in policy decisions.
 Illegal Orders (Section 41)
 Issuing directives beyond legal authority.

title: The Government of Union Territories Act, 1963.
description:
Governs the administration of all Union Territories in India
 Defines the powers and responsibilities of administrators
 Establishes legislative frameworks for UTs without legislatures
 Common Violations Under the Act:
 Administrative Overreach (Section 4)
 Exercise of powers beyond delegated authority
 Violation of administrative chain of command
 Financial Mismanagement (Section 17)
 Misappropriation of UT funds
 Unauthorized financial commitments
 Service Rule Violations (Section 21)
 Illegal appointments/promotions
 Breach of recruitment regulations
 Land and Property Irregularities (Section 12)
 Unauthorized land allotments
 Illegal change of land use

title: (Civil Service) Amendment Rules, 2015.
description:
The (Civil Service) Amendment Rules, 2015 are subordinate legislation under Article 309 of the Constitution, modifying:
 Recruitment conditions
 Service tenure rules
 Disciplinary procedures
 Promotion criteria
 Violations constitute:
 Administrative misconduct (Departmental proceedings)
 Criminal offenses when involving:
 Fraud (IPC 420)
 Forgery (IPC 467, 468, 471)
 Criminal breach of trust (IPC 409)
 Corruption (PC Act 1988)

title: The DANICS and DANIPS Rules, 2003.
description:
The Delhi, Andaman & Nicobar Islands Civil Service (DANICS) and Police Service (DANIPS) Rules, 2003 govern:
 Recruitment procedures
 Probation conditions
 Promotion criteria
 Disciplinary mechanisms
 for these All-India Services under Article 312 of the Constitution.
 Violations constitute:
 Service misconduct (Departmental action under Rule 8)
 Criminal offenses when involving:
 Forgery of service records (IPC 465/468)
 Corruption in appointments (PC Act 1988)
 Fraudulent claims (IPC 420)

title: The Rules of Business of the Government of Pondicherry, 1963.
description:
The Rules of Business, 1963 constitute the procedural framework governing:
 Transaction of government business
 Allocation of administrative responsibilities
 Document routing procedures
 Decision-making protocols
 for the Puducherry UT Administration under Article 239 of the Constitution.
 Violations constitute:
 Administrative irregularities (Departmental action)
 Criminal offenses when involving:
 Official document forgery (IPC 466)
 Abuse of official position (IPC 409)
 Corruption in approvals (PC Act 1988)
